feed.title: remco.space
feed.subtitle: Intergalactic space between Milliways, the TVA and the Galactic Center of the Universe.

entry.title: Automating a 2007 Peachtree Decco amplifier
entry.content:
The Peachtree Audio Decco (2007) is known for its excellent sound, and has two limitations. The USB input port is prone to breaking. Mine broke twice. Didn’t bother to fix it the second time. And volume and input selection need to be manually adjusted after powering on.

The first one has an easy answer: ignore the USB port and use a Raspberry Pi with a HiFiBerry DAC HAT into one of the analogue inputs instead.

The second one is the interesting one. Music starts playing, and the amplifier is off. Turning it on takes thirty seconds, because the analog tube needs to warm up. I have no patience to walk to the amplifier and turn it on and stand around for 30 seconds. Automate everything.

Two Raspberry Pis

The first version used two. A Pi Zero 2W with an infrared LED, impersonating the remote control. A Pi 3 running HiFiBerryOS with the DAC. The Zero logged into the Pi 3 over SSH to ask whether ALSA was playing anything, and if it was, blinked the amplifier awake.

It worked. It was also two computers doing one computer’s job. The obvious consolidation was to move the IR LED onto the Pi that already had the DAC, and that is where it stalls: HiFiBerryOS is built with buildroot, and a buildroot image contains what it was built with. LIRC is not in it. Adding LIRC means rebuilding the whole operating system.

So the consolidation went the other way. Not LIRC onto HiFiBerryOS: HiFiBerryOS off the Pi entirely. The Pi 3 now runs ordinary Raspberry Pi OS, where all of it is just a package. The HiFiBerry DAC, LIRC and the IR LED, and shairport-sync in a container for AirPlay. One Pi. No SSH.

The dev build of shairport-sync is the one running, pulled fresh every week. It works for me (tm), and is better than the stable version.

Inside the amplifier

There is room in the case, so that is where it went.

The IR LED points directly at the Decco’s own receiver from a few centimetres away, which makes the whole question of infrared range disappear. No transistor, no resistor, no line of sight across a room.

Nothing about the amplifier was modified. It still thinks it is being operated by its remote control, by someone with very consistent habits.

The remote configs

Both LIRC config files are in the repo, recorded from the original handsets:

  • decco.lircd.conf — the Peachtree Audio Decco remote
  • predac.lircd.conf — the Peachtree Audio preDac remote, a different amplifier

The Decco one is not mine to begin with. It started as the config Nick Touran published at Part of the Thing, who did the original capture and the irrecord work. Without that file this project would not have started at all. It had one flaw: holding volume made the motor drive stutter instead of sweeping. I asked in the comments there, years ago, whether LIRC’s repeat parameter was the answer, and never found out.

It was. My own remote was broken by then, so I soldered bodge wires across it, re-recorded it with irrecord, and the new capture came back with a repeat header the original does not have, and a gap of 108530 against the original’s

  1. The volume is smooth now. Both values are in decco.lircd.conf.

The preDac config is there because, as far as I can tell, no preDac LIRC config has ever been published anywhere.

Small things

Home Assistant can toggle the amplifier by hand.

Volume is set by firing VOL_UP at the amplifier eighteen times. Not exactly eighteen: the count is drawn from a Gaussian around it, so it is usually seventeen or nineteen.

When silence has run for a while, the amplifier switches to an unused input, then to another one, before shutting down. This is a warning shot. Nobody asked for it.

On every restart the Pi cycles the amplifier through all five inputs and back, to signal that it has restarted. The Pi reboots overnight, by which time the amplifier has been off for hours and is not listening. The signal is sent anyway, every day, to a switched-off amplifier.

The code, both configs, and the wiring are in Peachtree_Decco_hifiberry_IR.
entry.summary: Waking a Peachtree Audio Decco with a Raspberry Pi, a HiFiBerry DAC and an IR LED mounted inside the amplifier — and the LIRC configs for the Decco and preDac remotes.
